title: Play, Playfulness, Creativity and Innovation
description: The synthesis links playfulness with behavioural variability and exploratory recombination while distinguishing multiple forms and functions of play.
relevance: Broad comparative and boundary treatment of play.
scope: Play across animal behaviour, development and creativity
limitations: Wide-ranging narrative synthesis; mechanisms and causal evidence vary considerably by species and setting.
counterevidence: Play can be costly, constrained and non-creative; recurrence does not prove one function.
source_title: Play, Playfulness, Creativity and Innovation
bias_or_access_issue: Comparative examples are selective and human cultural diversity is incomplete.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer innateness, necessity or a single adaptive purpose from the recurrence of play.
notes: Cambridge scholarly book citation and DOI verified.

title: Habits in Everyday Life: Thought, Emotion, and Action
description: Frequently repeated actions in stable contexts were often performed with limited deliberation while thought and emotion continued alongside them.
relevance: Shows that much goal-relevant action is cue-linked rather than continuously chosen.
scope: Habitual behaviour in everyday adult life
limitations: Self-report and US convenience samples; habit frequency and automaticity are difficult to infer from retrospective reports.
counterevidence: Habitual action is not mindless action and can remain goal-compatible.
source_title: Habits in Everyday Life: Thought, Emotion, and Action
source_author_or_org: Wendy Wood; Jeffrey M. Quinn; Deborah A. Kashy
bias_or_access_issue: Daily routines of sampled populations may reflect specific institutions and resources.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer fixed character, absence of agency or universal habit prevalence.

title: How Are Habits Formed: Modelling Habit Formation in the Real World
description: Repeated performance in a stable cue context showed asymptotic growth in self-reported automaticity with substantial person-behaviour variation.
relevance: Empirical account of cue consistency and habit acquisition.
scope: Automaticity growth during repeated daily behaviour
limitations: Small self-selected UK sample, self-report and selected health behaviours; estimated times are not universal thresholds.
counterevidence: Missing one opportunity did not materially derail average formation, but trajectories varied widely.
source_title: How Are Habits Formed: Modelling Habit Formation in the Real World
source_author_or_org: Phillippa Lally; Cornelia H. M. van Jaarsveld; Henry W. W. Potts; Jane Wardle
bias_or_access_issue: Western volunteers and simple repeated behaviours limit transportability.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim habits form in a fixed number of days or that repetition guarantees persistence.

title: A New Look at Habits and the Habit-Goal Interface
description: Habits are proposed to be directly cued by recurring contexts and can persist despite shifts in current goals unless contexts or competing responses change.
relevance: Connects automaticity with environmental structure and goal pursuit.
scope: Habit activation, goals and context cues
limitations: Model integrates laboratory and field evidence but does not establish one mechanism for all repeated action.
counterevidence: Habits can serve valued goals and remain sensitive to major context disruption.
source_title: A New Look at Habits and the Habit-Goal Interface
source_author_or_org: Wendy Wood and David T. Neal
bias_or_access_issue: Evidence base is predominantly Western and task-specific.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not oppose habit and agency as mutually exclusive systems.

title: Implementation Intentions: Strong Effects of Simple Plans
description: Linking a situational cue to a specified response can increase cue accessibility and automate initiation of goal-directed action.
relevance: Mechanism joining deliberate goals to cue-triggered action.
scope: If-then planning and goal enactment
limitations: Selective narrative review; effects vary with goal commitment, cue encounter and outcome measurement.
counterevidence: Plans cannot create opportunity, skill or commitment and may be brittle when conditions change.
source_title: Implementation Intentions: Strong Effects of Simple Plans
bias_or_access_issue: Many experiments use Western convenience samples and short follow-up.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat planning as sufficient for control or goal attainment.

title: Implementation Intentions and Goal Achievement: A Meta-Analysis of Effects and Processes
description: Implementation intentions produced a medium-to-large average improvement in goal attainment across the reviewed experiments.
scope: If-then plans across goal domains
limitations: Publication bias, heterogeneous outcomes, variable study quality and older short-term trials constrain the pooled estimate.
counterevidence: Effects depend on sufficiently strong goals and identifiable cues; averages do not predict a specific setting.
source_title: Implementation Intentions and Goal Achievement: A Meta-Analysis of Effects and Processes
source_author_or_org: Peter M. Gollwitzer and Paschal Sheeran
bias_or_access_issue: Western sampling and researcher-defined outcomes dominate.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim a universal effect size or that plans overcome structural constraints.
notes: Publisher chapter page and DOI verified.

title: Building a Practically Useful Theory of Goal Setting and Task Motivation: A 35-Year Odyssey
description: Specific, difficult goals often improve task performance when commitment, ability, feedback and task conditions support pursuit.
relevance: Strong synthesis of goal mechanism and moderators.
scope: Specific challenging goals and performance
limitations: Much evidence comes from work and laboratory settings; outcomes emphasise performance and assigned tasks.
counterevidence: Complex tasks, weak commitment, missing feedback and inappropriate goals reduce or reverse effects.
source_title: Building a Practically Useful Theory of Goal Setting and Task Motivation: A 35-Year Odyssey
source_author_or_org: Edwin A. Locke and Gary P. Latham
bias_or_access_issue: Organisational and North American evidence is prominent.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that harder goals are always better or that performance improvement equals broader agency.

title: Goals Gone Wild: The Systematic Side Effects of Overprescribing Goal Setting
description: Narrow or excessive goals can produce tunnel vision, risk taking, unethical behaviour, distorted learning and damaged intrinsic motivation.
relevance: Required contrary source preventing goal-pursuit idealisation.
scope: Failure modes of specific and difficult goals
limitations: Argumentative review with selective examples rather than a formal meta-analysis; prevalence of harms is unresolved.
counterevidence: Goal effects depend on design, monitoring, incentives and task ecology.
source_title: Goals Gone Wild: The Systematic Side Effects of Overprescribing Goal Setting
source_author_or_org: Lisa D. Ordóñez; Maurice E. Schweitzer; Adam D. Galinsky; Max H. Bazerman
bias_or_access_issue: Many examples come from organisations and US institutions.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not conclude that goal setting is generally harmful.

title: The need to belong: Desire for interpersonal attachments as a fundamental human motivation
description: A wide range of findings is consistent with a recurrent motive to form and maintain durable positive relationships.
scope: Evidence across social and personality psychology on stable interpersonal bonds
limitations: Narrative synthesis predates many contemporary replication and cross-cultural standards; evidence is heterogeneous and much is Western.
counterevidence: Need for connection does not specify which relationships or groups people seek and does not entail harmony.
source_title: The need to belong: Desire for interpersonal attachments as a fundamental human motivation
source_author_or_org: Roy F. Baumeister and Mark R. Leary
bias_or_access_issue: English-language psychology and US-heavy evidence dominate.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer a unitary innate need, universal intensity, or that belonging causes every associated outcome.
notes: Foundational synthesis; accepted with modern scope limits.

title: Perceived social isolation and cognition
description: Perceived isolation is associated with altered attention, interpretation and expectations in social situations, suggesting feedback between felt disconnection and social behaviour.
relevance: Distinguishes subjective belonging from mere proximity.
scope: Adult evidence on perceived isolation, social cognition and self-protective processing
limitations: Many pathways are correlational; causal direction and cultural transportability are unresolved.
counterevidence: Objective network size and subjective isolation are non-equivalent; solitude is not necessarily harmful.
source_title: Perceived social isolation and cognition
source_author_or_org: John T. Cacioppo and Louise C. Hawkley
bias_or_access_issue: Evidence is concentrated in industrialised populations and uses culturally specific loneliness measures.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not diagnose isolated people as cognitively biased or infer that social contact alone resolves isolation.
notes: Boundary source for simplistic inclusion claims.

title: The social self: On being the same and different at the same time
description: Group identification may help reconcile motives for inclusion and differentiation; identification is predicted to vary with how categories satisfy both.
relevance: Explains why belonging and differentiation can coexist.
scope: Social identity and optimal distinctiveness in mainly Western experimental literature
limitations: Primarily theoretical and based on Western-developed constructs; not a universal motive model.
counterevidence: People can sustain overlapping identities and do not always prefer intermediate-sized or distinctive groups.
source_title: The social self: On being the same and different at the same time
bias_or_access_issue: Constructs may privilege individualist understandings of distinctiveness.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim a biologically fixed optimal group size or universal balance point.
notes: Useful mechanism, treated as model rather than settled fact.

title: Social identity complexity
description: Representing memberships as cross-cutting rather than fully overlapping can be associated with broader tolerance toward outgroups.
scope: How multiple group memberships are cognitively represented
limitations: Much evidence is correlational and Western; complexity measures may not travel cleanly across cultural contexts.
counterevidence: Multiple identities can also conflict, become hierarchically ordered, or be politically suppressed.
source_title: Social identity complexity
source_author_or_org: Sonia Roccas and Marilynn B. Brewer
bias_or_access_issue: Formal identity measures may miss relational and place-based identities.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not assume that adding identities mechanically reduces exclusion.
notes: Boundary against one-group models of identity.

title: Social categorization and intergroup behaviour
description: Arbitrary categorisation under constrained laboratory conditions was sufficient to produce average ingroup-favouring allocations.
scope: Minimal-group allocation experiments with British school participants
limitations: Artificial task, narrow sample, demand and interpretation concerns; does not establish durable hostility or adult cross-cultural universality.
counterevidence: Minimal categories can produce weak, absent or context-dependent effects and often show ingroup favour rather than outgroup harm.
source_title: Social categorization and intergroup behaviour
source_author_or_org: Henri Tajfel, Michael G. Billig, Robert P. Bundy and Claude Flament
bias_or_access_issue: British male youth and historically specific experimental setting.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer innate tribalism, inevitable conflict, or that all categorisation causes prejudice.
notes: Accepted as mechanism probe, not naturalistic prevalence evidence.

title: The minimal group paradigm: Theoretical explanations and empirical findings
description: Minimal-group discrimination is replicable under some conditions, but its size and interpretation depend on norms, task structure and explanatory assumptions.
relevance: Contrary and boundary source for categorisation claims.
scope: Review of minimal-group evidence and competing explanations
limitations: Older literature; limited cultural coverage and publication-bias assessment.
counterevidence: Norm-following, fairness expectations and experimenter-defined choices compete with pure social-identity explanations.
source_title: The minimal group paradigm: Theoretical explanations and empirical findings
bias_or_access_issue: Predominantly Western laboratory research.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat minimal-group findings as a single-process demonstration of natural group hostility.

title: The group as the container of generalized reciprocity
description: Ingroup favouritism was linked to expectations of indirect reciprocity within groups rather than mere category attachment alone.
scope: Japanese experiments testing expectations and ingroup cooperation
limitations: Laboratory choices and Japanese samples limit transportability; mechanism is not uniquely established.
counterevidence: When reciprocity expectations are removed, favouritism can weaken.
source_title: The group as the container of generalized reciprocity
bias_or_access_issue: Single-country experimental tradition.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not reduce all identification to strategic exchange or generalise the Japanese mechanism universally.

title: Mere membership in minimal groups does not necessarily produce ingroup favoritism
description: Categorisation alone is not a sufficient explanation of favouritism; norms, identification, interdependence and task meaning shape effects.
relevance: Direct contrary source against categorical inevitability.
scope: Review of minimal-group paradigms and moderators
limitations: Review conclusions depend on heterogeneous paradigms and are not a formal meta-analysis.
counterevidence: Some minimal-group studies do find favouritism, so the boundary is conditional rather than null.
source_title: Mere membership in minimal groups does not necessarily produce ingroup favoritism
source_author_or_org: Jim A. C. Everett, Nadira S. Faber and Molly Crockett
bias_or_access_issue: Literature remains heavily WEIRD.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim that categories are inert or that minimal-group evidence is entirely artifactual.
notes: Title-level claim retained only with conditional wording.

title: A meta-analytic test of intergroup contact theory
description: Intergroup contact was generally associated with lower prejudice across varied groups and settings, with stronger designs still showing an association.
relevance: Shows group boundaries are modifiable rather than fixed.
scope: Hundreds of studies of intergroup contact and prejudice
limitations: Residual selection and publication biases remain; effects vary and most evidence comes from structured modern institutions.
counterevidence: Contact can fail or backfire under threat, inequality, coercion or negative interaction.
source_title: A meta-analytic test of intergroup contact theory
source_author_or_org: Thomas F. Pettigrew and Linda R. Tropp
bias_or_access_issue: Western and institutional samples are overrepresented.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim any contact reduces exclusion or that contact alone removes structural inequality.

title: The contact hypothesis re-evaluated
description: The strongest causal evidence for durable contact effects is thinner and less generalisable than the broad contact literature suggests.
relevance: Adversarial boundary on optimistic boundary-change claims.
scope: Randomised and quasi-randomised intergroup-contact interventions
limitations: Eligible rigorous studies cover limited populations, interventions and outcomes.
counterevidence: Some well-designed contact programmes do reduce prejudice; critique concerns evidential strength and transportability, not impossibility.
source_title: The contact hypothesis re-evaluated
source_author_or_org: Elizabeth Levy Paluck, Seth A. Green and Donald P. Green
bias_or_access_issue: Many target groups and Global South settings remain understudied.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not dismiss contact or equate weak evidence with evidence of no effect.

title: Does multiple categorization reduce intergroup bias?
description: Cross-cutting category information reduced some forms of bias under specified experimental conditions by disrupting simple us-them classification.
relevance: Mechanism for permeable and cross-cutting boundaries.
scope: Experiments on crossing and combining social categories
limitations: Short-term laboratory outcomes and Western samples; effects depend on salience and representation.
counterevidence: Multiple categories can preserve or intensify bias when aligned, hierarchical or threatening.
source_title: Does multiple categorization reduce intergroup bias?
source_author_or_org: Richard J. Crisp, Miles Hewstone and Mark Rubin
bias_or_access_issue: Artificial categories and student samples dominate.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that identity complexity automatically produces tolerance.

title: The psychology of prejudice: Ingroup love or outgroup hate?
description: Ingroup favouritism does not necessarily entail hostility; outgroup harm typically requires additional conditions such as threat, competition or moral exclusion.
relevance: Critical distinction between affiliation and antagonism.
scope: Social-psychological evidence distinguishing ingroup attachment from outgroup hostility
limitations: Predominantly Western evidence and conceptual synthesis; conditions are not exhaustive.
counterevidence: Ingroup institutions can nevertheless produce exclusion indirectly through unequal allocation and closure.
source_title: The psychology of prejudice: Ingroup love or outgroup hate?
bias_or_access_issue: Research often measures attitudes rather than material power.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not sanitise ingroup favouritism or deny that preferential allocation can sustain inequality.
notes: Backbone source for non-inevitability of hostility.

title: The coevolution of cultural groups and ingroup favoritism
description: Arbitrary cultural markers can become linked to conditional cooperation when people assort and update strategies.
relevance: Connects boundary markers to coordination conditions.
scope: Marker-based coordination experiments and models with adult participants
limitations: Experimental ecology is stylised; modelling assumptions do not establish historical evolution.
counterevidence: Markers need not sustain favouritism under all payoff and information structures.
source_title: The coevolution of cultural groups and ingroup favoritism
source_author_or_org: Charles Efferson, Rafael Lalive and Ernst Fehr
bias_or_access_issue: Swiss participant base and abstract experimental setting.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that visible differences naturally or inevitably become ethnic boundaries.

title: Institutions, parasites and the persistence of in-group preferences
description: Stronger institutions and lower material insecurity were associated with weaker reliance on close-group favouritism, suggesting ecology and institutions condition parochial preference.
relevance: Non-WEIRD cross-national evidence on contextual variation.
scope: Cross-national variation in ingroup preference using World Values Survey measures
limitations: Country-level observational design, contested proxies and ecological inference prevent individual causal conclusions.
counterevidence: Not all measures or models support the same mechanism; national averages conceal internal diversity.
source_title: Institutions, parasites and the persistence of in-group preferences
source_author_or_org: Daniel J. Hruschka and Joseph Henrich
bias_or_access_issue: Survey construct equivalence and country coverage are imperfect.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim parasites or institutions directly cause individual prejudice.

title: National parochialism is ubiquitous across 42 nations around the world
description: Average cooperation favoured compatriots across participating nations, but effect magnitudes varied substantially across countries and situations.
relevance: Broad non-WEIRD comparison of one identity boundary.
scope: Cooperation decisions involving national ingroups and outgroups across 42 countries
limitations: Online/experimental samples, nation as a particular modern identity, and uneven representativeness limit generalisation.
counterevidence: Ubiquity in sampled national comparisons does not imply all identities, persons or behaviours show parochialism.
source_title: National parochialism is ubiquitous across 42 nations around the world
source_author_or_org: Angelo Romano, Daniel Balliet, Toshio Yamagishi and Junhui Wu
bias_or_access_issue: Nation-state categories and internet access structure the sample.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer innate nationalism, universal outgroup hostility or prehistoric equivalents of nations.
notes: Recurrence evidence with explicit institution-specific scope.

title: Rethinking individualism and collectivism: Evaluation of theoretical assumptions and meta-analyses
description: Individualism and collectivism are multidimensional, context-sensitive constructs; country and ethnicity contrasts are smaller and less uniform than common binaries imply.
relevance: Boundary on culturally simplistic identity models.
scope: Cross-national and ethnic-comparison literature on individualism and collectivism
limitations: Measures are heterogeneous and comparison literature overrepresents a small set of countries.
counterevidence: Some average cultural differences remain meaningful, but direction varies by component.
source_title: Rethinking individualism and collectivism: Evaluation of theoretical assumptions and meta-analyses
source_author_or_org: Daphna Oyserman, Heather M. Coon and Markus Kemmelmeier
bias_or_access_issue: Nation and ethnicity are crude proxies; within-group variation is substantial.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not classify persons or societies as simply individualist or collectivist.

title: Differences between tight and loose cultures: A 33-nation study
description: Norm strength and sanctioning vary across countries and are associated with ecological and historical conditions.
relevance: Cross-cultural evidence that conformity conditions vary.
scope: Perceived norm strength and tolerance of deviance across 33 nations
limitations: Observational country-level associations, measurement equivalence concerns and within-country heterogeneity prevent causal or person-level inference.
counterevidence: Tightness is not equivalent to collectivism, conformity in every domain or moral superiority.
source_title: Differences between tight and loose cultures: A 33-nation study
bias_or_access_issue: National samples and country averages obscure minorities and subcultures.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat societies as fixed cultural personalities or infer ecology causes norm strength.

title: Social networks and cooperation in hunter-gatherers
description: Cooperators clustered in Hadza social networks, and camp formation offered opportunities for assortment beyond close kin.
relevance: Non-WEIRD field evidence on affiliation beyond kin.
scope: Hadza camp networks and public-goods behaviour in Tanzania
limitations: Cross-sectional network data cannot cleanly separate selection, influence and shared environment; one population cannot represent hunter-gatherers generally.
counterevidence: Kinship still mattered, and clustering does not establish a universal cooperation mechanism.
source_title: Social networks and cooperation in hunter-gatherers
source_author_or_org: Coren L. Apicella, Frank W. Marlowe, James H. Fowler and Nicholas A. Christakis
bias_or_access_issue: Research categories and elicitation methods may not fully capture Hadza relational meanings.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer ancestral universality or claim that networks independently cause cooperation.

title: Co-residence patterns in hunter-gatherer societies show unique human social structure
description: Camps commonly included many non-kin and affinal links, creating broad networks beyond the nuclear family.
relevance: Cross-cultural anchor on flexible kin/non-kin affiliation.
scope: Residential composition across 32 mobile hunter-gatherer societies
limitations: Ethnographic records vary in quality and historical period; mobile foragers are diverse and not direct models of ancestral humanity.
counterevidence: Kin relations remain important and residence does not disclose subjective belonging or all cooperation.
source_title: Co-residence patterns in hunter-gatherer societies show unique human social structure
bias_or_access_issue: Colonial disruption and researcher coding affect records.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not equate contemporary foragers with the human past or infer one natural family form.

title: Characterization of hunter-gatherer networks and implications for cumulative culture
description: Fluid multilevel networks connected households and camps through kin and non-kin ties, enabling information flow across local groups.
relevance: Non-WEIRD comparative evidence on multilevel affiliation.
scope: Agta and BaYaka multilevel social networks in the Philippines and Congo Basin
limitations: Two populations, observational networks and model-based implications limit causal and historical claims.
counterevidence: Network connectivity does not imply equal access, absence of conflict or a universal band structure.
source_title: Characterization of hunter-gatherer networks and implications for cumulative culture
bias_or_access_issue: Field measures may privilege observable contact over locally meaningful ties.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer a fixed evolved network architecture or prove that networks caused cumulative culture.

title: The substance of kinship and the heat of the hearth: Feeding, personhood, and relatedness among Malays in Pulau Langkawi
description: Relatedness was understood and enacted partly through co-residence, feeding and shared substance, challenging a universal reduction of kinship to genealogy.
relevance: Anthropological anchor on culturally constituted kinship.
scope: Kinship and relatedness in a Malay community on Langkawi
limitations: Intensive single-site ethnography is interpretive and not prevalence evidence; the account is historically situated.
counterevidence: Genealogy and marriage still matter; constructed relatedness does not mean kin categories are arbitrary.
source_title: The substance of kinship and the heat of the hearth: Feeding, personhood, and relatedness among Malays in Pulau Langkawi
bias_or_access_issue: Translation, ethnographer position and later social change delimit the account.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not generalise Langkawi concepts to Malays or humanity, or deny biological relations any relevance.

title: Kin and kinship psychology both influence cooperative coordination in Yasawa, Fiji
description: Both genealogical relatedness and culturally recognised kin categories helped predict coordination, indicating that biological and cultural kinship information can jointly matter.
relevance: Non-WEIRD field evidence integrating genealogy and cultural recognition.
scope: Cooperative decisions and locally recognised kinship in Yasawa communities, Fiji
limitations: Observational and task-based evidence from one regional setting cannot establish causal mechanisms or universality.
counterevidence: Effects are context-specific and do not make all kin ties cooperative or all non-kin ties weak.
source_title: Kin and kinship psychology both influence cooperative coordination in Yasawa, Fiji
source_author_or_org: Rita A. McNamara and Joseph Henrich
bias_or_access_issue: Locally elicited categories improve fit but translation and researcher modelling remain.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer an innate kin module, genetic determinism or a universal kinship taxonomy.
notes: Direct bridge between kinship form and cooperative expectation.

title: The Psychology of Social Status
description: Status is socially conferred respect and influence and is associated with behaviour, cognition and well-being.
relevance: Defines status and separates it from adjacent constructs.
scope: social-psychological status literature
limitations: The literature is heterogeneous and substantially correlational.
counterevidence: Status is distinct from power and material resources and is not valued identically in every setting.
source_title: The Psychology of Social Status
source_author_or_org: Cameron Anderson; John Angus D. Hildreth; Laura Howland
bias_or_access_issue: Evidence is disproportionately Western and based on organisations or temporary groups.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat status striving as universal, unitary or biologically fixed.

title: Social Hierarchy: The Self-Reinforcing Nature of Power and Status
description: Power and status are distinct bases of rank that can become self-reinforcing through expectations and behaviour.
relevance: Connects rank allocation to institutional reinforcement.
scope: power and status in groups and organisations
limitations: Many mechanisms are inferred from laboratory and organisational studies.
counterevidence: Hierarchies can change and power need not bring respect.
source_title: Social Hierarchy: The Self-Reinforcing Nature of Power and Status
source_author_or_org: Joe C. Magee; Adam D. Galinsky
bias_or_access_issue: Formal organisations and US samples dominate.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim every hierarchy is stable, functional or legitimate.
notes: Power concerns resource dependence; status concerns esteem.

title: The Evolution of Prestige: Freely Conferred Deference as a Mechanism for Enhancing the Benefits of Cultural Transmission
description: The paper proposes prestige as voluntarily conferred deference toward valued expertise, distinct from coercive dominance.
relevance: Foundational distinction between coercion and valued influence.
scope: prestige and social learning
limitations: The evolutionary account is theoretical and several predictions were initially indirect.
counterevidence: Prestige and dominance may coexist; deference can follow institutional position or visibility rather than expertise.
source_title: The Evolution of Prestige: Freely Conferred Deference as a Mechanism for Enhancing the Benefits of Cultural Transmission
bias_or_access_issue: Ethnographic examples are selective and evolutionary assumptions are difficult to test.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not present the prestige model as proven evolutionary history or a complete taxonomy of rank.

title: Two Ways to the Top: Evidence That Dominance and Prestige Are Distinct Yet Viable Avenues to Social Rank and Influence
description: Dominance and prestige were empirically distinguishable and both predicted perceived influence in the studied groups.
relevance: Empirical anchor for multiple routes to influence.
scope: small groups and social perception
limitations: Small-group settings and measured perceptions do not represent all institutions or cultures.
counterevidence: The strategies are not exhaustive and may be misperceived or combined.
source_title: Two Ways to the Top: Evidence That Dominance and Prestige Are Distinct Yet Viable Avenues to Social Rank and Influence
source_author_or_org: Joey T. Cheng; Jessica L. Tracy; Tom Foulsham; Alan Kingstone; Joseph Henrich
bias_or_access_issue: Samples were mainly Western and status criteria were locally narrow.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not call dominance and prestige universal exhaustive routes to rank.

title: Prestige and Dominance: A Review of the Dual Evolutionary Model of Social Hierarchy
description: Evidence broadly supports distinctions among rank-acquisition strategies, rank dimensions, attention, influence and copying.
relevance: Boundary-setting review of the dominant model.
scope: dual-strategies evidence
limitations: Constructs and levels of analysis are often conflated and evidence for ultimate fitness claims is limited.
counterevidence: Authority, expertise, likability and coercion can overlap; copying is not exclusive to prestige.
source_title: Prestige and Dominance: A Review of the Dual Evolutionary Model of Social Hierarchy
source_author_or_org: K. Redhead; J. Cheng; C. Driver; T. Foulsham; R. O'Gorman
bias_or_access_issue: Much evidence comes from Western groups and self-report measures.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not collapse strategy, reputation, influence and reproductive outcome into one mechanism.

title: Dominance and Prestige: Dual Strategies for Navigating Social Hierarchies
description: Dominance and prestige involve different behaviours and interpersonal consequences, with context shaping their use.
scope: hierarchy-navigation strategies
limitations: Brief review; evidence base is uneven across outcomes.
counterevidence: Institutional authority and mixed strategies complicate a binary account.
source_title: Dominance and Prestige: Dual Strategies for Navigating Social Hierarchies
source_author_or_org: Jon K. Maner; Charleen R. Case
bias_or_access_issue: Western workplace and laboratory evidence is prominent.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer stable personality types or inevitable sex differences.
notes: Use as synthesis rather than causal proof.

title: Power, Approach, and Inhibition
description: The approach-inhibition model proposes that power changes attention, affect and action partly through reduced dependence and constraint.
relevance: Makes power central while retaining conditionality.
scope: psychological effects of power
limitations: A broad theory assembled from mixed evidence; later findings are not uniformly consistent.
counterevidence: Power can increase responsibility or perspective-taking under some identities, roles and accountability conditions.

title: Power and Perspectives Not Taken

title: Egalitarian Behavior and Reverse Dominance Hierarchy

relevance: Key anthropological boundary to hierarchy inevitability.

counterevidence: Egalitarianism can coexist with gender, age or ritual inequalities and does not eliminate informal prestige.

bias_or_access_issue: Ethnographic archive and researcher categories may obscure local meanings.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not describe egalitarian societies as hierarchy-free or infer ancestral human nature.

title: Egalitarian Societies
description: Material arrangements, mobility and sharing practices are associated with different capacities to resist durable dependence and hierarchy.
scope: immediate-return and delayed-return hunter-gatherer societies
limitations: Typology simplifies diverse histories and is not a causal experiment.
counterevidence: Foragers are not uniformly egalitarian; gender and age inequalities may persist.
source_title: Egalitarian Societies
bias_or_access_issue: Colonial disruption and selective ethnographic access constrain comparison.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not equate subsistence mode with a fixed political order or pristine past.

title: Universal and Variable Leadership Dimensions Across Human Societies
description: Some leadership dimensions recur across societies while their content, authority and pathways vary substantially.
relevance: Broad non-WEIRD comparison of rank criteria.
scope: ethnographic leadership across 60 societies
limitations: Ethnographic coding cannot establish causal mechanisms or exhaustive local categories.
counterevidence: Leadership may be situational, constrained or weak; recurrence does not prove universality.

prohibited_overextension: Do not infer innate leadership traits or one natural hierarchy.

title: The Content and Structure of Reputation Domains Across Human Societies: A View from the Evolutionary Social Sciences
description: Reputation is multidimensional, commonly tracking locally valued competence, prosociality and threat-related qualities.

prohibited_overextension: Do not claim one universal status metric or evolutionary origin from recurrence.

title: Why Do Men Seek Status? Fitness Payoffs to Dominance and Prestige

relevance: Detailed non-WEIRD case linking rank dimensions to outcomes.

counterevidence: Male-focused measures omit women's and other status systems; local pathways may not transport.
source_title: Why Do Men Seek Status? Fitness Payoffs to Dominance and Prestige

downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer universal male motives, genetic fitness strategies or natural patriarchy.

title: Leadership in an Egalitarian Society
description: Influence was associated with multiple qualities and operated without unrestricted coercive authority.
relevance: Shows rank without consolidated coercive power.

limitations: Cross-sectional field measures cannot resolve selection, influence or historical change.
counterevidence: Community size and institutions alter leadership; influence is not equivalent to command.

source_author_or_org: Christopher von Rueden; Michael Gurven; Hillard Kaplan; Jonathan Stieglitz
bias_or_access_issue: Male visibility and local translation shape recorded leadership.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not universalise Tsimane leadership or call egalitarianism absence of rank.

title: It's a Wonderful Life: Signaling Generosity Among the Ache of Paraguay
description: Productive generosity may provide reputational and social returns alongside consumption sharing.
relevance: Connects contribution, signalling and rank in a non-WEIRD setting.

prohibited_overextension: Do not call generosity disguised selfishness or a universal mating strategy.

description: Shame intensity covaried with anticipated social devaluation across varied communities.

source_title: Shame Closely Tracks the Threat of Devaluation by Others, Even Across Cultures
bias_or_access_issue: Translation and scenario equivalence remain concerns.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not label shame innate, universally adaptive or uniformly beneficial.

title: The Psychological Structure of Pride: A Tale of Two Facets
description: Pride judgments separated into achievement-linked and grandiosity-linked clusters with different correlates.
relevance: Adds emotion and self-evaluation to status maintenance.

source_author_or_org: Jessica L. Tracy; Richard W. Robins
bias_or_access_issue: North American samples and English emotion terms dominate.

title: Knowing Who's Boss: Implicit Perceptions of Status from the Nonverbal Expression of Pride
description: Pride displays influenced automatic status judgments in experimental tasks.
relevance: Shows signalling can shape allocated status.
scope: status inferences from pride displays
limitations: Static displays and laboratory categorisation have limited ecological validity.
counterevidence: Status signals are interpreted through culture, context and stereotypes and can be strategically performed.

prohibited_overextension: Do not treat body displays as transparent biological readouts of rank.

title: Why Status Matters for Inequality
description: Widely shared status beliefs can coordinate expectations and reproduce categorical inequality in interaction.
relevance: Connects micro-level deference to macro-level inequality.

counterevidence: Material resources, law, organisations and resistance also produce or disrupt inequality.

downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not reduce structural inequality to interpersonal beliefs or imply disadvantaged groups consent.

title: Groups Reward Individual Sacrifice: The Status Solution to the Collective Action Problem
description: Groups accorded more status to members perceived as making costly contributions, which supported further contribution.
relevance: Shows reciprocal loop between contribution and conferred rank.
scope: contribution and status allocation in groups
limitations: Laboratory groups and stylised tasks do not establish durable institutional effects.
counterevidence: Contribution can be coerced, misrecognised or rewarded according to unequal norms.

bias_or_access_issue: US participant samples and visible contributions limit transportability.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only

title: Income Inequality, Equality of Opportunity, and Intergenerational Mobility
description: Higher inequality is associated with lower intergenerational mobility across compared countries, with institutions mediating pathways.
relevance: Links rank structures to opportunity and persistence.
scope: income inequality and mobility across countries
limitations: Small country samples and measurement differences prevent simple causal inference.
counterevidence: Country averages conceal within-country variation and mobility has multiple dimensions.
source_title: Income Inequality, Equality of Opportunity, and Intergenerational Mobility
bias_or_access_issue: Administrative coverage and income definitions differ.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim inequality mechanically causes immobility or that income captures all status.

title: Where Is the Land of Opportunity? The Geography of Intergenerational Mobility in the United States
description: Mobility varies substantially across US areas and is associated with segregation, inequality, family structure, social capital and schools.
relevance: Shows institutional and geographic patterning of mobility.
scope: geographic variation in US intergenerational income mobility
limitations: Associations do not identify causal effects of all area characteristics.
counterevidence: US income mobility is one historically specific dimension of rank movement.

bias_or_access_issue: Tax records omit some populations and non-economic forms of mobility.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not attribute mobility differences to individual merit or one community characteristic.

title: Prestige Bias in Cultural Evolutionary Models: Current Evidence and Outstanding Questions
description: Evidence for copying prestigious models is mixed and depends on how prestige, success, familiarity and expertise are operationalised.
relevance: Strong boundary source for prestige and deference claims.
scope: prestige-biased social learning
limitations: Studies use heterogeneous proxies and often cannot separate prestige from success bias.
counterevidence: Learners may ignore, contest or strategically defer to prestigious models.
source_title: Prestige Bias in Cultural Evolutionary Models: Current Evidence and Outstanding Questions
source_author_or_org: Charlotte O. Brand; Alex Mesoudi; Thomas J. H. Morgan
bias_or_access_issue: Laboratory and Western samples remain prominent despite some broader evidence.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat prestige bias as automatic, universal or proof of evolutionary origin.
notes: Prevents the prestige theory becoming unfalsifiable.

title: Allostasis: A model of predictive regulation
description: Regulation often anticipates needs and coordinates bodily resources rather than relying only on reactive error correction.
relevance: Directly frames homeostasis and anticipatory regulation.
scope: Predictive physiological regulation in humans and other animals
limitations: Primarily a conceptual synthesis; several mechanisms and therapeutic extensions are not independently tested in this article.
counterevidence: Feedback homeostasis remains important; predictive control does not replace every local regulatory mechanism.
source_title: Allostasis: A model of predictive regulation
bias_or_access_issue: Biomedical and laboratory literatures dominate.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat allostasis as a complete or uniquely proven theory of bodily regulation.

title: The concept of allostasis in biology and biomedicine
description: Organisms maintain stability through changing physiological and behavioural responses whose costs depend on life history and environment.
relevance: Connects biomedical and comparative regulation while preserving ecological variation.
scope: Energy regulation and responses to predictable and unpredictable demands across organisms
limitations: Framework-level evidence does not isolate one causal mechanism or quantify human universality.
counterevidence: Species ecology and individual condition alter regulatory ranges and costs.
source_title: The concept of allostasis in biology and biomedicine
source_author_or_org: Bruce S. McEwen and John C. Wingfield
bias_or_access_issue: Comparative coverage is selective and translation to humans is indirect.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer identical mechanisms across species or an evolved human psychological drive from comparative recurrence.

title: The reactive scope model: A new model integrating homeostasis, allostasis, and stress
description: Normal regulation includes predictive and reactive ranges; overload and failure depend on mediator-specific and individual thresholds.
relevance: Provides a direct conceptual boundary on allostatic claims.
scope: Physiological mediator ranges in humans, laboratory animals and free-living animals
limitations: The model is heuristic and requires mediator-specific empirical tests.
counterevidence: It was proposed partly because allostasis and stress terminology can be ambiguous and overextended.
source_title: The reactive scope model: A new model integrating homeostasis, allostasis, and stress

downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only

title: Protective and damaging effects of stress mediators
description: Stress mediators can support short-term adaptation while repeated or poorly regulated activation can contribute to cumulative physiological cost.
relevance: Supports the adaptive-cost tension in bodily regulation.
scope: Human stress physiology and disease pathways
limitations: Narrative review; disease pathways and biomarkers do not identify an individual's cause or prognosis.
counterevidence: The same mediator can have protective or damaging effects depending on timing, dose and context.

bias_or_access_issue: Clinical evidence is concentrated in industrialised populations.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only

title: Acute stressors and cortisol responses: A theoretical integration and synthesis of laboratory research

downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only

title: Psychological stress and disease

relevance: Bounds claims about long-term regulatory cost.
scope: Psychological stress and selected human disease processes

downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only

title: Using neuroscience to help understand fear and anxiety: A two-system framework

title: When fear is near: Threat imminence elicits prefrontal-periaqueductal gray shifts in humans

title: Dopamine reward prediction-error signalling: A two-component response

title: Emotional expressions reconsidered: Challenges to inferring emotion from human facial movements

title: Culture and the categorization of emotions

title: Mapping expressive differences around the world: The relationship between emotional display rules and individualism versus collectivism

source_title: Mapping expressive differences around the world: The relationship between emotional display rules and individualism versus collectivism
bias_or_access_issue: Student and urban samples plus binary cultural dimensions compress diversity.

title: Ideal affect: Cultural causes and behavioral consequences
description: People may value different affective states, and these ideals can diverge from the states they actually experience.
relevance: Distinguishes affective valuation from affective occurrence.
scope: Culturally valued affective states and their behavioural correlates
limitations: Evidence was concentrated in East Asian and European-American comparisons and often correlational.
counterevidence: Within-group heterogeneity and situational demands limit broad cultural profiles.
source_title: Ideal affect: Cultural causes and behavioral consequences
bias_or_access_issue: Bicultural, class and regional variation are incompletely represented.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer actual feeling, personality or need from cultural membership.

title: Culture, emotion, and well-being: Good feelings in Japan and the United States
description: Associations between positive feeling, social engagement and well-being differed across the two studied cultural contexts.
relevance: Non-identical affective organisation provides a boundary on universal models.
scope: Reported emotional experience and well-being in Japanese and US samples
limitations: Two-country self-report comparison cannot represent either culture fully or establish causality.
counterevidence: Common positive and negative affective experiences remained, while their relational organisation differed.
source_title: Culture, emotion, and well-being: Good feelings in Japan and the United States
source_author_or_org: Shinobu Kitayama, Hazel R. Markus and Masaru Kurokawa
bias_or_access_issue: Student/convenience samples and translation constrain equivalence.
downstream_use: Stage 1B synthesis and Stage 1R reconciliation only
prohibited_overextension: Do not turn Japanese-US average differences into universal East-West types or individual predictions.

title: Six-domain architecture for reconciliation
description: Stage 1R may provisionally compare evidence through six non-exhaustive domains; this is an analytical frame, not an empirical architecture.
scope: Analytical integration of the eight reviewed workstreams; not a species-wide ontology.
limitations: The six-domain grouping is broad, non-exhaustive and may absorb unlike mechanisms or obscure alternative classifications.
counterevidence: Construct non-equivalence, uneven coverage and domain-specific heterogeneity challenge a single architecture.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not call the domains exhaustive, universal, innate, modular or empirically established as the correct architecture.
revision_trigger: Broader sampling fails to recover the domains, construct non-equivalence prevents comparison, or the grouping obscures mechanisms.
notes: Rationale: reconciliation needs a shared comparison frame. Alternatives: organise by discipline, mechanism, life domain or source tradition. Consequence if wrong: false convergence, omission or obscured mechanisms. Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Embodied regulation is anticipatory and conditional
description: Regulation includes anticipatory and reactive adjustment whose consequences vary with timing, intensity, prior condition and environment.
scope: Human allostatic and stress studies plus bounded comparative regulatory models represented in W1.
limitations: Allostasis is a theoretical framework; evidence mixes species, biomarkers and heterogeneous stress contexts.
counterevidence: Reactive-scope and measurement critiques reject one universal threshold or transparent biomarker interpretation.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not use allostasis as a total explanation or biomarkers as individual truth signals.
revision_trigger: New mechanistic or cross-cultural evidence materially changes the anticipatory-regulation synthesis.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Threat and reward are decomposable
description: Defensive response, conscious fear, wanting, liking and reward learning are related but partly separable.
scope: Reviewed defence, fear, incentive-salience and reward-learning paradigms.
limitations: Construct overlap, task impurity and mixed human/animal evidence limit clean architectural claims.
counterevidence: Measurement reviews show labelled tasks do not always isolate the intended process.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer fixed circuitry, motive or universal subjective experience from one task or signal.
revision_trigger: Replicated evidence collapses or materially redraws the claimed dissociations.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Control constructs and cue-sensitive action are distinguishable
description: The reviewed literature distinguishes beliefs about means and ends, executable control and actual opportunity, and separately reports cue-sensitive repeated action and implementation plans.
scope: Reviewed control-belief, planning, habit and cross-cultural autonomy evidence, mainly Western and organisational/health contexts.
limitations: Theory-heavy and correlational evidence; agency constructs may privilege Western authorship and independence.
counterevidence: Cross-cultural autonomy studies show endorsed obligation can coexist with agency and challenge independence-based measures.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not reduce agency to independence, confidence, intention, compliance or a stable trait.
revision_trigger: Cross-cultural or causal evidence materially changes the control-related synthesis.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Boundedness is supported; strategy transport remains uncertain
description: Decision-making in the reviewed tasks operates under limited attention, information and computational capacity; the transport and value of particular heuristics or copying strategies remains uncertain.
scope: Reviewed attention, framing, heuristic and social-learning paradigms.
limitations: Boundedness is better supported than claims about which heuristic or copying strategy is adaptive; non-WEIRD testing is limited.
counterevidence: Ecological-rationality evidence shows information-reducing strategies can outperform exhaustive methods in some environments.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not create a universal bias inventory, cultural hierarchy or innate copying module.
revision_trigger: Cross-cultural tests or improved models materially alter boundedness or strategy conclusions.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Affiliation recurs without a natural group unit
description: Across the reviewed settings, social networks commonly included overlapping kin and non-kin ties; categories could alter expectations without inherently producing hostility.
scope: Reviewed Western identity studies and a limited set of contemporary small-scale and cross-cultural network studies.
limitations: Settings are not globally representative and contemporary small-scale communities do not reconstruct ancestral organisation.
counterevidence: Minimal categorisation does not consistently produce hostility; cross-cutting identities and contact modify outcomes.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer inevitable tribalism, one natural kinship form or a specialised coalition module.
revision_trigger: Broader network or construct-equivalence evidence changes the overlap/boundary conclusion.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Reviewed literatures distinguish multiple routes to influence
description: Prestige, dominance, authority, inherited position and resource control are distinguished routes to influence whose conversion can vary with audiences and institutions.
scope: Reviewed organisational, experimental and ethnographic literature on visible status and influence.
limitations: Taxonomies are theoretically loaded; coverage is uneven across societies, genders and public versus domestic domains.
counterevidence: Levelling practices and hidden gendered or ritual inequality challenge both unitary hierarchy and simple egalitarian accounts.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat the listed currencies as exhaustive, hierarchy as inevitable or rank as merit.
revision_trigger: Broader gendered, domestic or cross-cultural evidence materially changes status distinctions.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Rank is reproduced and resisted
description: Visibility, reputation and resource access can reinforce one another, while coalitions, refusal, mobility and levelling practices can constrain domination.
scope: Documented organisational, experimental and ethnographic settings where rank feedback or levelling was observed.
limitations: The complete visibility-reputation-deference-resource sequence is not established across settings; selection and prior resources remain alternatives.
counterevidence: Mobility, refusal, coalitions and sharing rules can constrain domination; apparent equality may conceal other inequalities.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim all rank self-reproduces, all resistance succeeds or visible equality removes hidden inequality.
revision_trigger: Longitudinal or comparative evidence materially changes feedback or resistance claims.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Observed cooperation varies with institutional conditions
description: Observed cooperation varied with expectations, boundaries, reciprocity and rules in the reviewed paradigms; this does not exclude stable dispositions.
scope: Reviewed laboratory games, field cases and limited cross-societal cooperation studies.
limitations: Stylised games and Western pools are overrepresented; stable dispositions and unmeasured histories remain possible.
counterevidence: Outcomes vary across societies and designs; no single reciprocity, punishment or participation mechanism is sufficient.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer universal selfishness, benevolence or one sufficient institutional recipe.
revision_trigger: New field or cross-cultural evidence identifies a robust general mechanism.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Punishment has conditional collective effects
description: Punishment can support or damage collective outcomes depending on the norm, sanction structure, power distribution and retaliation.
scope: Reviewed punishment and contribution paradigms plus limited field evidence.
limitations: Most outcomes are contribution, payoff or welfare measures rather than moral evaluations; transportability is limited.
counterevidence: Punishment increased contribution in some designs but reduced welfare, targeted cooperators or provoked retaliation in others.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not equate punishment with justice, cooperation, legitimacy or innateness.
revision_trigger: Broader field evidence materially changes conditional outcome patterns.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Western meaning research distinguishes multiple judgements
description: Primarily Western meaning research distinguishes related judgements of coherence, direction or purpose, and mattering; equivalent organisation elsewhere is unresolved.
scope: Primarily Western self-report research on coherence, direction/purpose and mattering.

title: Measurement choices shape visible and comparable variation
description: Translation, instrument design, sampling and construct equivalence affect which psychological differences become visible or comparable.
scope: Cross-cultural measurement-invariance, translation and psychometric evidence in W8.
limitations: Measurement caution does not determine the substantive causes of observed differences or eliminate real variation.
counterevidence: Some constructs show partial invariance and useful comparability at bounded levels.

title: Recurrence does not establish innateness
description: Cross-context recurrence is compatible with preparedness, common developmental demands, convergent learning, diffusion, institutional reproduction and artefact.
scope: Methodological inference rule governing Stage 1B and Stage 1R, not a substantive property of humans.
limitations: The rule does not rank all alternative explanations or prove any particular alternative.
counterevidence: Convergent developmental, comparative and mechanistic evidence could support stronger origin claims for a bounded construct.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not reverse the rule into a claim that biology is absent or every pattern culturally unconstrained.
revision_trigger: Revise when discriminating evidence supports or rejects a specific origin account.
notes: Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.

title: Power audit at reconciliation
description: Stage 1R should test accounts of hierarchy, cooperation, duty and meaning by identifying power, beneficiaries, casualties and credible resistance.
scope: Stage 1R review protocol only; not a human-baseline finding.
limitations: A power-first lens may underweight coordination benefits, shared commitments or non-power explanations.
counterevidence: Reasonable critics may prefer functional, consensual or plural explanatory priorities.
downstream_use: Candidate Stage 1B component finding for Stage 1R only
prohibited_overextension: Do not present the value commitment as empirical law or assume every asymmetry is illegitimate.
revision_trigger: Revisit if the protocol systematically distorts evidence or suppresses plausible alternatives.
notes: Value basis: prevent naturalisation of hierarchy and hidden extraction. Trade-off: may overweight power. Likely disagreement: functionalist, consensual and value-plural perspectives. Component gate disposition: approved_with_cautions; see STAGE_REVIEW.md and PASS_FORWARD.md.
